Go back

A complete system for urban last-mile delivery. Not a single object but a set of things designed to work together: a compact vehicle, a modular cargo bay, an onboard collaborative robot, a wearable exoskeleton, a new parcel label language, and an app. The whole is called Me-Moe: Mezzo Express Multifunzione Operativa Essenziale.

The brief

Urban proximity logistics fails in three predictable places: vehicles too large for city streets, local restrictions that fragment the working day, and deliveries that collapse when no one is home. The project addresses all three without reinventing the vehicle from scratch, working instead at the level of the cargo module, the tools inside it, and the service wrapped around it.

Oversized vehicles
Standard vans obstruct urban mobility. The right format sits between a scooter and a light van: compact enough for restricted zones, large enough to carry a meaningful load.
Local restrictions
ZTL zones, narrow delivery windows, and environmental regulations require a vehicle that complies by default, not by workaround.
Recipient availability
Every failed delivery multiplies cost and emissions. The system needs to decouple the delivery from the moment the recipient happens to be home.

The cargo module

The cargo bay is the structural core of the project. Designed as a standardised unit independent from any specific chassis, it can be retrofitted onto existing vehicles through a simple attachment system. Every component can be positioned in any order and repeated across a mixed fleet. The module is also stackable; a second unit can sit on top of the first where ceiling clearance allows.

Access points were completely reorganised. Two rear doors open the full width of the bay. A lateral sliding door gives access mid-route without repositioning. A motorised floor rail moves packages toward the opening automatically. The result is a loading and unloading sequence that no longer requires the driver to climb inside.

The bay is built on a tubular steel frame with composite panel cladding. The base houses the battery pack in the electric configuration. Interior shelving runs on standardised rails shared with the cobot cart, making the robot and shelf units interchangeable in position.

Bay width
1530 mm
Bay height
1371 mm
Total length
2035 mm
Track width
931 mm

AGADEXO

Repetitive heavy lifting is the leading cause of long-term injury in delivery work. AGADEXO is a wearable exoskeleton designed as a structural component of the vehicle rather than a stored accessory. It replaces the passenger seat entirely.

A single hydraulic arm activates the moment the cab door opens. The exoskeleton pivots outward and descends to standing height. The driver steps into it without bending down or reaching inside. Door closes. Mechanism resets. Under ten seconds.

Door closed
Hydraulic extends
Driver steps in
Assisted load
> 30 kg
Quick releases
Shoulder · Torso · Arm
Deploy time
under 10 sec

Three quick-release buckles at the shoulder, torso and arm allow emergency removal at any point. The safety system is redundant by design; any single buckle is sufficient to disengage the unit completely. Integration was studied against the AGADEXO shoulder unit by Agade and the ABB SWIFTI CRB 1100 cobot.

The parcel label

Existing shipping labels were designed entirely for machine reading. Dense, visually undifferentiated, and difficult to parse at a glance; they work for sensors but not for people. A new label system was designed from scratch around a chromatic coding logic compliant with ISO 3864 and OSHA 1910.145.

Regular
#89C273
Fragile
#FCC046
Temp.
#006FB9
Urgent
#D70911
Hazard
#2D2926
Top
Delivery type in large format. Readable from across the bay.
Middle
Company logo, order code, sender and recipient addresses.
Bottom
ISO-compliant condition icons and barcode for automated reading.
Coordinated tape
A matching adhesive tape repeats the order code across all seams in the same colour. The parcel is identifiable even when stacked or partially covered.

The app

The service around Me-Moe was designed as a complete UX flow: booking, confirmation and tracking. The mental model is ride-hailing applied to parcels. The user does not fill a shipping form; they book a vehicle.

Booking
Destination, parcel type, weight and dimensions go in first. The app shows available Me-Moe units filtered by proximity and time slot. One tap confirms.
Tracking
Live vehicle position on a map with current route and estimated arrival. Standard hub-to-hub tracking tells you where the parcel has been. This tells you where it is right now.
Flows
Book · Track · Return
Login
Google · Microsoft · Meta
Palette
Hot pink · Lime · Teal

The process

The project moved from a SWOT analysis of the proximity logistics sector through vehicle sizing, cargo bay structure, technology integration, label design and digital service design. Each layer was built in response to a specific failure identified in the research phase.

Modelling and rendering in Blender. Graphic and label design in Adobe Illustrator. Layout in Adobe InDesign. FLUX.1 for contextual reference imagery.

Supervised by Consuelo Nava with co-supervisors Carmine L. Quistelli and Domenico Lucanto.
